skill.name: dast-zap
skill.description: Guides authorized dynamic application security testing with OWASP ZAP for web applications, APIs, authenticated flows, CI pipelines, and vulnerability reporting.
skill.summary: Run authorized OWASP ZAP DAST scans and interpret web application security results.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command, network, filesystem, and secret-related matches, but most are documented OWASP ZAP examples and CI templates. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found. The real risk is that the skill enables active security scanning and executable automation, so users need authorization and review before use.

medium_findings.title: Authorized Active DAST Traffic Required
medium_findings.description: The skill provides active OWASP ZAP scan guidance and an automation job that can send attack traffic to target applications. This is legitimate security testing, but it can disrupt systems or violate policy if run without written authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly describe active scanning and include an activeScan job. The risk depends on user authorization, so this is a confirmed operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Executable CI and Docker Templates Need Review
medium_findings.description: The bundled CI templates run Docker-based ZAP scans and install parsing tools in pipeline environments. The commands are expected for this skill, but marketplace users should review targets, mounts, and permissions before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static command findings map to real shell commands in CI templates. They appear purposeful and not obfuscated, but they execute in user infrastructure and warrant review.

low_findings.title: Secret References Are Environment Placeholders
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged API key and credential references. The reviewed files use environment-variable placeholders for ZAP and test authentication rather than hardcoded secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows placeholder variables such as TEST_PASSWORD and ZAP_API_KEY. No literal credential value was found in the reviewed lines.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Paths Appear in Test Payload Documentation
low_findings.description: The sensitive file detections are examples for XXE and path traversal verification. They do not show the skill reading local files or collecting host credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths appear inside labeled vulnerability test payload examples. The context supports a false-positive classification for local credential access.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Example Targets
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings use example.com, target-app.com, staging hosts, or official documentation links. These are examples and references, not hidden outbound endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are visible configuration examples and documentation references. I did not find evidence of covert network destinations.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many Docker, shell, and CI examples for running OWASP ZAP. They are transparent examples, but users should treat them as executable instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and aligned with DAST usage. The risk is execution impact, not hidden command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Scanning Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill and assets define targets for ZAP crawling, passive scanning, active scanning, and API scanning. These templates can generate network traffic against configured systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly configure scan scope and active scan behavior. This is expected for the skill but requires authorized targets.

content.value_statement: Web teams need repeatable runtime security checks before releases. This skill guides authorized OWASP ZAP scans, authenticated testing, CI setup, and report review.
actual_capabilities: Explains baseline, full, API, and authenticated OWASP ZAP scan workflows.
Provides Docker command patterns for passive and active DAST scans.
Includes GitHub Actions and GitLab CI templates for automated scanning.
Maps common ZAP findings to OWASP Top 10 and CWE categories.
Guides false positive review, report generation, and security gate thresholds.
limitations: Requires explicit authorization before scanning any target application.
Does not replace manual penetration testing or exploit validation.
Needs OWASP ZAP, Docker, and network access configured by the user.
Scan quality depends on target scope, authentication, and application coverage.

use_cases.title: Pre-release web scan
use_cases.description: Run baseline and active ZAP checks against staging before approving a release.

use_cases.title: Pipeline security gate
use_cases.description: Add ZAP scan templates to CI and fail builds on high-risk findings.

use_cases.title: Authenticated API assessment
use_cases.description: Test protected REST, GraphQL, or SOAP APIs with scoped credentials and generated reports.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a baseline scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a staging site
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an authorized OWASP ZAP baseline scan for my staging web application. Include scope, command options, and report outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Add CI scanning
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CI plan for OWASP ZAP scanning in GitHub Actions or GitLab. Include artifacts, thresholds, and safe target handling.

prompt_templates.title: Configure authenticated testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through an authenticated OWASP ZAP scan for a form-login application. Use environment variables for credentials and explain validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review scan results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me triage OWASP ZAP findings by risk, false-positive likelihood, OWASP Top 10 mapping, and remediation priority.

output_examples.input: I need to scan a staging web app before release.
output_examples.output: A scoped baseline scan plan with target rules, safe timing, report formats, and a reminder to confirm authorization.

output_examples.input: I want ZAP in GitLab CI.
output_examples.output: A pipeline outline with a ZAP container job, saved reports, threshold checks, and manual controls for active scans.
A triage summary that groups findings by risk, suggests validation steps, and maps issues to remediation owners.

best_practices: Scan only systems where you have written authorization and a defined scope.
Start with passive baseline scans before enabling active attack checks.
Store credentials in secret managers or environment variables, never in templates.
anti_patterns: Running active scans against production without approval or rate limits.
Committing ZAP context files that contain real usernames, passwords, or tokens.
Failing builds on every alert before reviewing false positives and risk context.

faq.question: Does this skill run OWASP ZAP by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates. The user controls execution, targets, credentials, and infrastructure.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is active scanning safe for production?
faq.answer: Active scanning can send attack traffic. Use it only with approval, rate limits, and a clear maintenance plan.

faq.question: Does it support API testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers OpenAPI, GraphQL, SOAP, authentication, and API report workflows.

faq.question: How should credentials be handled?
faq.answer: Use environment variables or a secret manager. Do not commit real credentials in ZAP configuration files.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate security tooling, but it includes executable templates and active network scanning workflows.
